content:
# Numbers Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 158-159 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0119 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_009_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_009_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### The Passover in the Wilderness (vv. 1-5)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, saying,

**2** "Let the Sons of Israel keep the Passover at its appointed time.

**3** On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you shall keep it at its appointed time; according to all its statutes and according to all its ordinances you shall keep it."

**4** And Moses spoke to the Sons of Israel to keep the Passover.

**5** And they kept the Passover in the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai; according to all that YHWH had commanded Moses, so the Sons of Israel did.

---

### The Second Passover (vv. 6-14)

**6** And there were men who were unclean through a dead body of a man, and they could not keep the Passover on that day. And they came before Moses and before Aaron on that day.

**7** And those men said to him, "We are unclean through a dead body of a man. Why are we kept back, so as not to bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time among the Sons of Israel?"

**8** And Moses said to them, "Stand still, and I will hear what YHWH will command concerning you."

**9** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**10** "Speak to the Sons of Israel, saying, 'If any man of you or of your generations shall be unclean through a dead body, or shall be on a distant journey, he shall still keep the Passover to YHWH.

**11** In the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight, they shall keep it; with unleavened bread and bitter herbs they shall eat it.

**12** They shall leave none of it until morning, and they shall not break a bone of it; according to all the statute of the Passover they shall keep it.

**13** But the man who is clean and is not on a journey, and refrains from keeping the Passover, that soul shall be cut off from his people, because he did not bring the offering of YHWH at its appointed time; that man shall bear his sin.

**14** And if a sojourner shall sojourn among you and would keep the Passover to YHWH, according to the statute of the Passover and according to its ordinance, so shall he do. You shall have one statute, both for the sojourner and for the native of the land.'"

---

### The Cloud over the Tabernacle (vv. 15-23)

**15** And on the day that the tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the tabernacle, the tent of the testimony, and at evening there was upon the tabernacle as it were an appearance of fire until morning.

**16** So it was continually: the cloud covered it, and an appearance of fire by night.

**17** And whenever the cloud was taken up from over the tent, after that the Sons of Israel would set out; and in the place where the cloud settled, there the Sons of Israel would encamp.

**18** At the mouth of YHWH the Sons of Israel would set out, and at the mouth of YHWH they would encamp. All the days that the cloud rested upon the tabernacle they would remain encamped.

**19** And when the cloud lingered upon the tabernacle many days, then the Sons of Israel kept the charge of YHWH and did not set out.

**20** And sometimes the cloud was a number of days upon the tabernacle; according to the mouth of YHWH they would remain encamped, and according to the mouth of YHWH they would set out.

**21** And sometimes the cloud was from evening until morning, and the cloud was taken up in the morning, and they would set out; or a day and a night, and the cloud was taken up, and they would set out.

**22** Whether it was two days, or a month, or a longer time, when the cloud lingered upon the tabernacle, remaining upon it, the Sons of Israel would remain encamped and would not set out; but when it was taken up, they would set out.

**23** At the mouth of YHWH they would encamp, and at the mouth of YHWH they would set out. They kept the charge of YHWH, at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- Chronological note:** Numbers 9:1 is dated to "the first month of the second year," while Numbers 1:1 is dated to "the second month of the second year." Thus chapter 9 records an event that chronologically precedes the census of chapter 1. This is one of the clearest indications that the book of Numbers does not follow a strictly chronological arrangement. The Talmud (Pesachim 6b) takes this as the basis for the principle: "there is no early or late in the Torah" (אין מוקדם ומאוחר בתורה).

2. **Verse 1 -- "in the wilderness of Sinai" (בְּמִדְבַּר סִינַי):** The Leningrad Codex reads bemidbar sinai. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads εν τη ερημω Σινα (in the wilderness of Sina). Both sources agree in locating this pericope at Sinai prior to the departure.

3. **Verse 3 -- "at twilight" (בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם):** Literally "between the two evenings." This phrase has been debated since antiquity: the Pharisaic tradition interpreted it as the period between the sun's decline (after noon) and sunset, while the Sadducees and Samaritans understood it as the brief interval between sunset and full darkness. The LXX renders προς εσπεραν (toward evening). The formal rendering "at twilight" preserves the temporal sense without adjudicating the ancient dispute. The dual form (ha'arbayim) is retained in the idiomatic English.

4. **Verse 6 -- "unclean through a dead body of a man" (טְמֵאִים לְנֶפֶשׁ אָדָם):** Literally "unclean by the soul/life of a man," i.e., by contact with a human corpse. The word nephesh here carries the sense of a deceased person (cf. Numbers 6:6). The LXX reads ακαθαρτοι επι ψυχη ανθρωπου (unclean upon a soul of a man). The formal rendering preserves the causal construction of the Hebrew.

5. **Verse 7 -- "Why are we kept back" (לָמָּה נִגָּרַע):** The Niphal of גרע means "to be diminished, held back, deprived." The men's complaint is not mere inconvenience but a theological protest: exclusion from the Passover threatens their covenantal standing. The LXX renders ινα τι υστερησωμεν (why should we fall short). The formal translation follows the MT's passive construction.

6. **Verse 8 -- "Stand still" (עִמְדוּ):** The imperative of עמד. Moses does not presume to answer a case not previously legislated but waits for divine instruction. This verse reveals the mechanism of ongoing Torah legislation: new situations prompt new inquiry, and YHWH supplements existing law. The LXX reads στητε (stand).

7. **Verse 10 -- "a distant journey" (בְּדֶרֶךְ רְחֹקָה):** The Leningrad Codex has a punctum extraordinarium (extraordinary dot) above the letter he in the word רְחֹקָה. This is one of ten instances of dotted letters in the Torah, marked by the Masoretes to signal a textual or interpretive tradition. The dot may indicate uncertainty about whether the he is original, or it may mark a hermeneutical tradition that "distant" should be interpreted loosely -- the Mishnah (Pesachim 9:2) discusses what counts as "far." The LXX reads εν οδω μακραν (on a far road), without reflecting the scribal dot.

8. **Verse 12 -- "they shall not break a bone of it" (וְעֶצֶם לֹא יִשְׁבְּרוּ־בוֹ):** This Passover regulation, drawn from Exodus 12:46, acquires christological significance in the New Testament (John 19:36). The LXX reads οστουν ου συντριψουσιν απ αυτου (a bone they shall not break from it). The formal rendering follows the MT word order.

9. **Verse 13 -- "that soul shall be cut off" (וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא):** The karet penalty -- being "cut off" from one's people -- is the most severe penalty in the Pentateuch for ritual neglect. Its precise meaning (divine punishment, excommunication, childlessness, or premature death) was debated in rabbinic literature. The LXX reads εξολεθρευθησεται η ψυχη εκεινη (that soul shall be utterly destroyed). The Vaticanus reading intensifies the penalty with the compound verb.

10. **Verse 14 -- "one statute" (חֻקָּה אַחַת):** The legal parity between the sojourner (ger) and the native-born (ezrach) in matters of Passover observance is a consistent Pentateuchal principle (cf. Exodus 12:49; Leviticus 24:22; Numbers 15:16). The LXX reads νομος εις (one law). The formal rendering "one statute" follows the MT's term chuqqah rather than the LXX's nomos.

11. **Verse 15 -- "the tent of the testimony" (לְאֹהֶל הָעֵדֻת):** The tabernacle is here called "the tent of the testimony" (ohel ha'edut), a designation that emphasizes the ark and its tablets of testimony housed within. This differs from the more common "tent of meeting" (ohel mo'ed). The LXX reads τον οικον του μαρτυριου (the house of the testimony). The formal rendering preserves the MT's distinct terminology.

12. **Verse 15 -- "as it were an appearance of fire" (כְּמַרְאֵה־אֵשׁ):** The preposition ke (as, like) creates a simile rather than an identification: the phenomenon resembled fire but was not ordinary fire. The LXX reads ωσει πυρος (as if fire/like fire), similarly preserving the simile. This numinous description recalls the pillar of fire in Exodus 13:21-22.

13. **Verse 18 -- "at the mouth of YHWH" (עַל־פִּי יְהוָה):** Literally "upon the mouth of YHWH." This phrase occurs six times in verses 18-23, creating a powerful refrain that emphasizes complete submission to divine direction. The LXX renders δια προσταγματος κυριου (by the ordinance of the Lord), interpreting the anthropomorphism as a formal decree. The formal rendering preserves "mouth" as a literal equivalent of the Hebrew pi, maintaining the anthropomorphic image.

14. **Verse 20 -- "a number of days" (יָמִים מִסְפָּר):** Literally "days of number," meaning a few or countable days -- an indefinite but small quantity. The LXX reads ημερας αριθμω (days in number). The phrasing captures the variability of the cloud's duration.

15. **Verse 22 -- "a month, or a longer time" (חֹדֶשׁ אוֹ־יָמִים):** The Hebrew yamim here means "a longer period" or literally "days" in an extended sense. The LXX reads μηνα η ημερων πλειονων (a month or of more days). The context demands a progression: two days, a month, or an even longer period. The formal rendering "a longer time" follows the contextual sense of yamim as an indefinite extension.

16. **Verse 23 -- "by the hand of Moses" (בְּיַד־מֹשֶׁה):** The chapter concludes by specifying that YHWH's commands came through Moses as mediator. The phrase "by the hand of" (beyad) is an idiomatic expression for agency or instrumentality. The LXX renders δια χειρος Μωυση (through the hand of Moses). The formal rendering preserves the Hebrew idiom.

### Structural Observations
- Numbers 9 contains two distinct sections linked by the theme of divine authority over Israel's communal life: the Passover regulations (vv. 1-14) and the cloud guidance (vv. 15-23). The Passover section governs time (when Israel worships), while the cloud section governs space (where Israel moves). Together they portray a community entirely directed by YHWH in both its liturgical calendar and its geographical movements.
- The Passover narrative (vv. 6-14) presents a rare example of case-law formation within the Pentateuch. The unclean men bring an unprecedented situation to Moses, who in turn consults YHWH, and new legislation results. This pattern -- situation, inquiry, divine response -- provides a window into how the ancient Israelite legal tradition understood Torah as a living, expandable body of instruction.
- The cloud pericope (vv. 15-23) is marked by extensive repetition: the phrase "at the mouth of YHWH" occurs six times, and the verbs for setting out (נסע) and encamping (חנה) alternate in a rhythmic pattern. This deliberate literary repetition creates the effect of patient, unhurried obedience -- the people wait and move solely at YHWH's direction, whether for two days or many months.
- The "appearance of fire" at night (v. 15) and the "cloud" by day (vv. 16-22) continue the Exodus theophany tradition (Exodus 13:21-22; 40:34-38). The cloud-and-fire motif frames the entire wilderness period as a sustained divine presence, not a single event but an ongoing reality governing every movement of the camp.
